videoio-audio-mixer-whisper.problemNarration: Rendering frames is the easy part. A useful video also needs exact presentation timestamps, continuous narration, music and effects mixed without overlap, captions that follow the words, and transitions that still work when the viewer seeks or replays.

evidence.title: Media APIs expose timestamped frames
evidence.body: WebCodecs models decoded and encoded media as explicit video frames and audio data with timestamps.

evidence.title: Mixing is a timeline operation
evidence.body: FFmpeg audio filters combine, delay, trim, and normalize streams by their timing and sample properties.

videoio-audio-mixer-whisper.interventionNarration: VideoIO writes app-rendered frames at explicit timestamps and decodes exact frames later. AudioMixer places PCM tracks on one sample clock. Timed Whisper segments become subtitle files, and MorphTransition uses the same positions for scrubbing and replay.
videoio-audio-mixer-whisper.proofTitle: This episode is the running VideoIO sample
videoio-audio-mixer-whisper.proofNarration: The video you are watching follows this path. The Codename One renderer captures frames in bulk, sends them through VideoWriter with exact presentation times, and passes the AudioMixer result into the same writer. The MP4 on screen is the output of that code.
videoio-audio-mixer-whisper.capturesTitle: Decode this output back at exact times
videoio-audio-mixer-whisper.captureNarration: After VideoIO closes this episode, the same API decodes exact timestamps for verification. This frame sheet is the round-trip check, while SRT and VTT files beside the MP4 carry the timed transcript.
videoio-audio-mixer-whisper.victoryTitle: You just watched the API run
videoio-audio-mixer-whisper.victoryNarration: This is not a support check or a mock presentation. The episode itself is a VideoIO export: Codename One frames, AudioMixer narration, hardware H.264 encoding, decoded-frame verification, and caption files reproduced from one script in continuous integration.

heroJourney.difficulty: Renewals, refunds, grace periods, SKUs, store notifications, and client-readable keys cross stores and trust boundaries.
heroJourney.intervention: Commerce normalizes receipts into entitlements and degrades safely; Secrets centralizes rotation while preserving the server-only boundary.

device-integrity-and-app-review.proofNarration: The Java code fetches a nonce, requests the platform token, and forwards it without interpreting it. The review scheduler separately encodes launches, age, and cooldown as policy rather than scattered prompt calls.
device-integrity-and-app-review.captureNarration: This is the shipped fallback rating sheet for platforms without a native prompt. High ratings can continue to the store; lower ratings can reach a feedback listener instead of being discarded.
device-integrity-and-app-review.victoryTitle: Higher attack cost, lower user annoyance
device-integrity-and-app-review.victoryNarration: The developer gains one portable integrity surface and one restrained review policy, while keeping security decisions off the untrusted client and avoiding prompt spam.

privacy-first-analytics.thumbnailSubtitle: One local gate controls every provider before any event leaves the device.
privacy-first-analytics.openingNarration: Codename One builds native apps in Java from one codebase. Today, one analytics facade keeps the consent decision ahead of every provider and leaves the backend replaceable.
privacy-first-analytics.humanBeat: A developer wants to understand the product without silently reporting a user's first screen to a dead or unwanted vendor before the privacy prompt appears.
privacy-first-analytics.visualIdentity: Every event reaches a closed consent gate before it can fan out to any analytics provider.
privacy-first-analytics.bespokeVisualization: An event visibly stops at the gate, then flows to several interchangeable providers only after opt-in.

heroJourney.identity: Codename One and consent-first product analytics are introduced.
heroJourney.problem: The old one-vendor service reports immediately and points at an obsolete protocol.
heroJourney.difficulty: Consent categories, identifiers, multiple backends, migration, and native providers can leak policy into every call site.
heroJourney.intervention: One facade gates events on device before fanning out through an open provider SPI.

resolutionMap.solution: opt-in on-device consent gate
five providers behind one facade
open AnalyticsProvider SPI

privacy-first-analytics.codeExcerpt:
Analytics.addProvider(new MatomoAnalyticsProvider(url, 7));

Analytics.screen("Home", null); // dropped: no consent yet

Analytics.setConsent(AnalyticsConsent.builder()
        .analytics(true)
        .crashReporting(true)
        .build());

Analytics.screen("Home", null); // now delivered
privacy-first-analytics.problemTitle: The old API sent first and asked nothing
privacy-first-analytics.problemNarration: A single vendor was baked into the framework, its old endpoint disappeared, and initialization immediately reported activity. Consent, provider choice, and event vocabulary were tangled together.

evidence.title: Consent must control collection behavior
evidence.body: Google's consent mode changes storage and data-collection behavior according to the user's consent choices.

evidence.title: Self-hosted analytics also needs a consent path
evidence.body: Matomo documents consent requirements and APIs for tracking and cookie consent rather than assuming collection is always allowed.

privacy-first-analytics.interventionTitle: Make the gate run before the provider
privacy-first-analytics.interventionNarration: Analytics records granular consent locally and drops calls before any provider sees them. After opt-in, the facade fans out to GA4, Matomo, Firebase, Codename One Cloud, logging, or a provider the application owns.
privacy-first-analytics.proofTitle: The same call is silent, then delivered
privacy-first-analytics.proofNarration: The provider is registered first, but the Home screen event disappears until analytics consent is granted. The order is visible in application code and enforced inside the facade.
privacy-first-analytics.captureNarration: The real analytics console appears only after an app registers a provider and a user grants consent. Overview, trends, events, goals, segments, and flows then operate on the data that was actually allowed.
privacy-first-analytics.victoryTitle: Measure the product without owning the user
privacy-first-analytics.victoryNarration: The developer chooses the privacy posture and backend, can mix providers or self-host, and can reset the pseudonymous client ID when the user asks to be forgotten.

A failing build needs

heroJourney.identity: Codename One and cloud-build diagnosis are introduced.

heroJourney.difficulty: Framework artifacts, servers, SDKs, signing rules, dependencies, and application changes can all move independently.
heroJourney.intervention: build.cn1Version pins a released framework or selects master for immediate fix verification.
heroJourney.victory: The developer compares known states, verifies a fix quickly, and returns production to a released version.

problemDimensions: false expectation of full time travel

resolutionMap.solution: pin the last known good release
build against master for verification

resolutionMap.problem: false expectation of full time travel

versioned-builds-master.codeExcerpt:
# Reproduce the last known good framework
build.cn1Version=7.0.182

# Verify a fix that just landed
build.cn1Version=master

# Ship from a released version after verification

versioned-builds-master.problemTitle: Everything moved; only one variable should
versioned-builds-master.problemNarration: A cloud build includes application code, framework artifacts, native SDKs, signing, repositories, and server tooling. Without a controlled version comparison, every diagnosis begins as guesswork.

evidence.title: Maven versions are explicit dependency coordinates
evidence.body: Maven's dependency mechanism resolves artifacts by group, artifact, and version so a build can name the framework state it expects.

evidence.title: Reproducibility depends on more than source
evidence.body: Reproducible-build guidance records how timestamps, toolchains, and environment inputs can affect otherwise identical source builds.

versioned-builds-master.interventionTitle: Pin for diagnosis; use master for verification
versioned-builds-master.interventionNarration: A released value asks the cloud to use known framework artifacts. Master asks it to use the current development head after a fix lands. Neither claim freezes Xcode, Android rules, certificates, or external repositories.
versioned-builds-master.proofNarration: The project first pins the last known good release. If a fix lands, the same property selects master for device verification, then returns to a released version before production shipping.
versioned-builds-master.captureNarration: The article artwork and source property are the proof here. This feature controls the build, and the selected value identifies the framework state used for the comparison.
versioned-builds-master.victoryTitle: Replace archaeology with a short experiment
versioned-builds-master.victoryNarration: The developer narrows the regression, verifies an unreleased fix without waiting for a nightly package, and keeps master out of the production release path.
versioned-builds-master.outroTitle: Which build state do you need to reproduce?
versioned-builds-master.outroPrompt: Tell us whether the hard part is pinning, master verification, or native toolchain drift.

A crash report is useful
only if a developer can act on it
seamless-crash-protection.thumbnailTitle:
FROM NATIVE CRASH
TO READABLE ISSUE
seamless-crash-protection.thumbnailSubtitle: Store first, scrub on device, symbolicate with build artifacts, then deduplicate in GitHub.
seamless-crash-protection.openingNarration: Codename One builds native apps in Java from one codebase. Today, a crash survives a bad network and returns as a readable issue instead of an address or an email storm.
seamless-crash-protection.humanBeat: A developer receives a production crash from a device they do not own and needs enough evidence to fix it without asking the user to reproduce the failure.
seamless-crash-protection.visualIdentity: An opaque crash address passes through local storage, privacy scrubbing, cloud symbols, and lands as one readable GitHub issue.
seamless-crash-protection.bespokeVisualization: The crash path visibly improves the evidence at each stage while preserving delivery through retries.

heroJourney.identity: Codename One and production crash triage are introduced.
heroJourney.problem: Native addresses, obfuscated names, unreliable networks, and duplicate email make real crashes hard to act on.
heroJourney.difficulty: Useful symbols live with the build, reports may contain personal data, and the app may die before a network send finishes.
heroJourney.intervention: The client stores and scrubs first; the build cloud symbolicates; GitHub receives a deduplicated issue.
heroJourney.victory: The developer opens one issue with platform, version, occurrence count, fingerprint, and a readable stack.

resolutionMap.solution: server-side symbolication with matching build artifacts
write eventId and report to Storage before upload
on-device PiiScrubber plus eventId deduplication
resolutionMap.proof: the scrubber code and GitHub mapping capture

seamless-crash-protection.problemTitle: The crash arrived, but the evidence did not
seamless-crash-protection.problemNarration: A signal can leave only a hexadecimal address. Android names may be obfuscated. The network may vanish before upload, and repeated failures can bury the same defect in email. The user sees the crash; the developer sees fragments.

evidence.title: Apple crash reports require matching symbols
evidence.body: Apple documents symbolication as the step that maps machine addresses back to human-readable function names and source locations.

evidence.title: Sensitive data should stay out of logs
evidence.body: OWASP logging guidance identifies personal data, tokens, passwords, and secrets that should be removed, masked, or excluded.

seamless-crash-protection.interventionNarration: The app writes a fresh event identifier and report to Storage before any send, scrubs personal data locally, and retries on the next launch until the cloud confirms receipt. Matching mapping files or dSYM artifacts then turn native addresses into names before GitHub filing.
seamless-crash-protection.proofTitle: The privacy rule executes before install
seamless-crash-protection.proofNarration: The custom scrubber runs the default email and digit rules, replaces application-specific account identifiers, and is registered before CrashProtection installs. The same startup enables storage draining and future uploads.
seamless-crash-protection.captureNarration: The first capture is a real symbolicated iOS signal crash with version, platform, fingerprint, counts, and readable frames. The second maps an application package to the GitHub repository that owns its crash triage.
seamless-crash-protection.victoryTitle: Start from an issue, not a mystery
seamless-crash-protection.victoryNarration: The developer can triage a native failure from iOS, macOS, Windows, Linux, or deobfuscated Android inside the team's existing issue workflow, even when the original device was offline.
seamless-crash-protection.outroTitle: Which crash is hardest for your team to reproduce?
seamless-crash-protection.outroPrompt: Tell us the platform and the evidence you usually wish you had.
seamless-crash-protection.endScreenVideoTitle: App Attestation and App Review Without Trusting the Client

native-apple-watch-and-wear.visualIdentity: Shared Java logic narrows into a compact watch form, then splits into Core Graphics on watchOS and the Android renderer on Wear OS.
native-apple-watch-and-wear.bespokeVisualization: The story keeps shared application logic visible while the two native packaging and rendering paths remain honest and distinct.

heroJourney.identity: Codename One and wearable application development are introduced.
heroJourney.problem: A small companion experience usually demands separate watchOS and Wear OS projects.
heroJourney.difficulty: watchOS lacks the iOS renderer, watches have tight budgets, Wear OS packaging differs, and phone UI does not fit a wrist.
heroJourney.intervention: CN.isWatch selects a compact shared UI while each build uses its native target and renderer.
heroJourney.victory: The developer reuses business logic and Java APIs, ships both wearables, and designs only the screen that must differ.

resolutionMap.solution: one Java source base and CN.isWatch branch
Core Graphics backend inside a SwiftUI shell
a separate watchMain tree-shaking root

native-apple-watch-and-wear.problemTitle: A tiny feature inherited two entire ecosystems
native-apple-watch-and-wear.problemNarration: watchOS is not a smaller iOS screen: it lacks UIKit, Metal, and the iOS port's rendering path. Wear OS is Android, but packaging and form factor still differ. Meanwhile the watch has less memory, less CPU, and no room for a phone layout.

evidence.title: Apple Watch apps use their own app structure
evidence.body: Apple documents watchOS applications, complications, and interaction patterns as watch-specific experiences rather than scaled phone screens.

evidence.title: Wear OS applications declare watch capabilities
evidence.body: Android's Wear OS guidance describes watch hardware declarations, standalone behavior, and wearable-specific packaging.
evidence.sourceTitle: Package and Distribute Wear OS Apps

native-apple-watch-and-wear.interventionTitle: Share the application; respect the watch
native-apple-watch-and-wear.interventionNarration: CN.isWatch is true on both wearable targets, so the application chooses a compact form once. Wear OS uses the Android renderer. watchOS runs ParparVM-translated code through a Core Graphics backend hosted in a generated SwiftUI application.
native-apple-watch-and-wear.proofTitle: The screen branch is ordinary Java
native-apple-watch-and-wear.proofNarration: The watch path hides the toolbar and uses a compact vertical form while the phone path remains full size. A separate watchMain can become the compilation root so unreachable phone screens and libraries never enter the watch slice.
native-apple-watch-and-wear.captureNarration: This is real Codename One UI in the Apple Watch simulator, rendered through Core Graphics. The point is not to drag a phone form onto a wrist; it is to reuse the API and logic while deliberately designing the smaller screen.
native-apple-watch-and-wear.victoryTitle: Build the glance, not another product
native-apple-watch-and-wear.victoryNarration: The developer keeps shared models, networking, storage, and UI APIs, adds the small experience users need, and lets native watchOS and Wear OS build paths handle the platform work underneath.
native-apple-watch-and-wear.outroTitle: What belongs on your user's wrist?
native-apple-watch-and-wear.outroPrompt: Choose the one phone workflow that should become a ten-second watch interaction.

funding-open-source-without-the-bait-and.youtubeTitle: How Codename One Funds Open Source Without Lock-In
funding-open-source-without-the-bait-and.openingTitle:
Open source needs money
without taking freedom back
funding-open-source-without-the-bait-and.thumbnailTitle:
FUND THE WORK
KEEP THE FREEDOM
funding-open-source-without-the-bait-and.thumbnailSubtitle: The open project stays complete; optional services must earn adoption by being useful.
funding-open-source-without-the-bait-and.openingNarration: Codename One is an open source Java platform for native applications. Today, the subject is how the company funds new ports, APIs, and maintenance without making the free project worse.
funding-open-source-without-the-bait-and.humanBeat: A developer adopts an open platform for control, then worries that neglect or a later license change will turn years of application work into leverage against them.
funding-open-source-without-the-bait-and.visualIdentity: Two familiar dead ends—abandonment and lock-in—are replaced by a loop connecting an open project, optional paid value, and continued engineering.
funding-open-source-without-the-bait-and.bespokeVisualization: The business model is shown as a testable exchange: freedom remains in the core while paid services finance the next visible engineering result.

heroJourney.identity: Codename One and long-term open-source maintenance are introduced.
heroJourney.problem: Projects without revenue can decay, while companies with trapped users can take promised freedoms back.
heroJourney.difficulty: Maintainers, native ports, build infrastructure, security work, and support all cost money even when source code is free.
heroJourney.intervention: The project remains GPL with the Classpath Exception, while the company sells metered build capacity and optional integrated services.
heroJourney.victory: The developer keeps a forkable platform and ownership of the application; paid features must win through usefulness rather than sabotage.

problemDimensions: paid value that weakens the free project

resolutionMap.solution: metered build infrastructure and optional services
resolutionMap.proof: the shipped build cloud, analytics, and crash workflows

resolutionMap.solution: GPL with the Classpath Exception
resolutionMap.proof: the repository license and application-linking exception

resolutionMap.problem: paid value that weakens the free project
resolutionMap.solution: open provider APIs with replaceable backends
resolutionMap.proof: AnalyticsProvider can target first-party, third-party, self-hosted, or custom services

funding-open-source-without-the-bait-and.problemTitle: Free source does not pay the maintenance bill
funding-open-source-without-the-bait-and.problemNarration: Native platforms, cloud builders, security fixes, support, and compatibility work continue every week. Without revenue, maintenance slows. With lock-in, a company can instead weaken the free path and charge users to escape a dependency it created.

evidence.title: The Classpath Exception protects application linking
evidence.body: GNU Classpath explains how the exception permits linking independent modules with the library without placing the resulting application under the GPL.

evidence.title: Sustainable projects need explicit funding paths
evidence.body: Open Source Guides documents services, support, crowdfunding, and other ways maintainers can fund ongoing work rather than relying on invisible unpaid labor.

funding-open-source-without-the-bait-and.interventionTitle: Sell convenience and integration; keep the project whole
funding-open-source-without-the-bait-and.interventionNarration: Codename One keeps the framework, ports, and build tooling open under GPL with the Classpath Exception. The company charges for metered build-cloud capacity and optional services such as integrated analytics or crash handling that sit on open APIs.
funding-open-source-without-the-bait-and.proofTitle: The provider choice stays in application code
funding-open-source-without-the-bait-and.proofNarration: The same analytics facade can use Matomo, GA4, Firebase, logging, a custom backend, or Codename One's service. Selecting the first-party option helps fund the project; declining it does not remove the consent gate or the provider API.
funding-open-source-without-the-bait-and.captureNarration: The value is visible in shipped work: the analytics console, the portable vector map, and the syntax-aware Java editor all exist in the open platform. Paid integration must finance more work like this, not hide the foundational API.
funding-open-source-without-the-bait-and.victoryTitle: Make the paid option earn the next renewal
funding-open-source-without-the-bait-and.victoryNarration: The developer retains source access, application ownership, alternative providers, and a self-hostable toolchain. The company earns revenue only when its build capacity or integrated service saves enough work to be worth choosing.
funding-open-source-without-the-bait-and.outroTitle: What optional service would earn your trust?
funding-open-source-without-the-bait-and.outroPrompt: Name the integration that would save work without reducing your control.
funding-open-source-without-the-bait-and.endScreenVideoTitle: Privacy-First Analytics in Java: Consent Before Collection

game-builder-2d-platformer.youtubeTitle: Build a Playable 2D Java Platformer From Visual Level Data
game-builder-2d-platformer.openingTitle:
A platformer level should be
a place you can edit
game-builder-2d-platformer.thumbnailTitle:
DRAW THE LEVEL
CODE THE RULES
game-builder-2d-platformer.thumbnailSubtitle: Tiles, actors, and properties stay visible; Java adds the win, power-ups, and death behavior.
game-builder-2d-platformer.openingNarration: Codename One builds native applications and games in Java from one codebase. Today, Duke's Coffee Run goes from a visual level to a playable platformer while Java stays focused on the rules.
game-builder-2d-platformer.humanBeat: A game developer wants to move one platform or coffee cup and test the feel immediately instead of hunting coordinates and rebuilding the application.
game-builder-2d-platformer.visualIdentity: Scattered coordinate setters become a painted level, a readable game file, and a small Java rule set.
game-builder-2d-platformer.bespokeVisualization: The level editor, saved scene data, and runtime rules stay visibly connected throughout the story.

heroJourney.identity: Codename One and a two-dimensional Java platformer are introduced.
heroJourney.problem: Hand-placing every sprite in source hides the level until runtime and makes each visual tweak a rebuild.
heroJourney.difficulty: Animation frames, tiles, parallax layers, gravity, collision, enemies, pickups, camera, and winning must agree.
heroJourney.intervention: Game Builder stores the scene as editable data and GameSceneView supplies explicit arcade behavior plus overridable hooks.
heroJourney.victory: The developer changes the level visually and writes only the distinctive rules for goals, power-ups, checkpoints, and death.

resolutionMap.solution: paint tiles and place named actors in Game Builder
resolutionMap.proof: the editor scene and play-test captures

resolutionMap.solution: explicit setArcadeBehavior(true)
protected update, pickup, and hit hooks

game-builder-2d-platformer.problemTitle: Coordinates are not a level editor
game-builder-2d-platformer.problemNarration: When sprite positions, terrain, enemies, and pickups live as setters in Java, nobody can see the level until it runs. Moving art forces a rebuild, designers cannot work safely, and behavior becomes tangled with layout.

evidence.title: Tile maps keep repeated level structure editable
evidence.body: Godot's TileMap guidance treats tile placement, collision, navigation, and painting as visual level-authoring data rather than scattered source coordinates.

evidence.title: Scene data and scripts solve different problems
evidence.body: Tiled's documentation stores maps, layers, objects, and custom properties as map data that game code can load and interpret.

game-builder-2d-platformer.interventionTitle: Let the editor own the place
game-builder-2d-platformer.interventionNarration: Game Builder saves layers, tile art, actors, transforms, and typed properties in a plain game file. The generated companion class binds names such as player, enemy, and flag to Java fields, while explicit arcade behavior enables gravity, running, jumping, patrols, pickups, collision, and camera follow.
game-builder-2d-platformer.proofTitle: The unique rule fits in one method
game-builder-2d-platformer.proofNarration: The constructor loads the visual scene and turns on the behavior that matched the preview. The update hook only checks whether Duke overlaps the named flag, then ends the level with the score.
game-builder-2d-platformer.captureNarration: The editor shows the finished terrain, background, player, coffee, exception monster, goal, and scene properties together. The play view then runs that saved scene as a platformer instead of reconstructing it from hidden coordinates.
game-builder-2d-platformer.victoryTitle: Iterate on the level without rewriting the game
game-builder-2d-platformer.victoryNarration: The developer can move art, change gravity, add pickups, or repaint terrain as data. Java stays with the rules the editor cannot infer: winning, power-ups, checkpoints, hit points, and death.
game-builder-2d-platformer.outroTitle: Which platformer rule should we add next?
game-builder-2d-platformer.outroPrompt: Choose a checkpoint, power-up, boss, moving platform, or death screen.
game-builder-2d-platformer.endScreenVideoTitle: Build a Blackjack Game in Java With Game Builder

the-codename-one-game-builder.youtubeTitle: Why Game Levels Should Be Visual Data, Not Java Coordinates
the-codename-one-game-builder.openingTitle:
The runtime was ready
The level was still invisible
the-codename-one-game-builder.thumbnailSubtitle: Draw level data visually, load one runtime model, and keep Java for behavior.
the-codename-one-game-builder.openingNarration: Codename One builds native applications and games in Java from one codebase. Today, one visual scene format turns the gaming runtime into an authoring workflow for two-dimensional, board, and three-dimensional games.
the-codename-one-game-builder.humanBeat: A developer has sprites, physics, input, and sound, but still cannot judge a level until dozens of object coordinates compile and run.
the-codename-one-game-builder.visualIdentity: A wall of object setters collapses into one plain game file that fans out into platformer, board, and dungeon scenes.
the-codename-one-game-builder.bespokeVisualization: The same data model visibly serves three kinds of game while a separate Java lane carries only application behavior.

heroJourney.identity: Codename One and visual game authoring are introduced.
heroJourney.problem: A portable runtime still leaves levels invisible when every object is created and positioned in code.
heroJourney.difficulty: Different game types need layers, assets, properties, projections, terrain, preview, and eventually streaming worlds.
heroJourney.intervention: Game Builder edits one plain scene model and generates companion bindings for the runtime.
heroJourney.victory: The developer draws 2D, board, or 3D levels, reviews them as data, and keeps Java focused on the rules.

resolutionMap.solution: hierarchy, asset library, canvas, and inspector
one GameLevel data model for 2D, board, and 3D
chunk and region streaming around the camera
resolutionMap.proof: StreamingTerrain and RegionProvider described in the article

the-codename-one-game-builder.problemTitle: A game engine without authoring still hides the game
the-codename-one-game-builder.problemNarration: A loop, sprites, physics, input, and sound can all be portable while the level remains dozens of constructors and position setters. Every visual change needs compilation, and data that artists should edit is mixed with runtime rules.

evidence.title: Scenes organize reusable game data
evidence.body: Godot's scene system composes nodes into reusable trees so spatial structure can be authored and instantiated separately from gameplay scripts.

evidence.title: Map editors preserve layers, objects, and properties
evidence.body: Tiled exports visual maps with layers, object data, tile sets, and custom properties that runtimes can load independently of editor code.

the-codename-one-game-builder.interventionTitle: Make the level a project artifact
the-codename-one-game-builder.interventionNarration: Game Builder stores layers, assets, objects, transforms, terrain, and typed properties in a plain game file. The runtime realizes that file into a Scene, and generated fields bind named objects without overwriting the constructor or update logic the developer owns.
the-codename-one-game-builder.proofTitle: One loader, three kinds of scene
the-codename-one-game-builder.proofNarration: The companion class loads a GameLevel resource into GameSceneView and starts the rules loop. The same model supports a side-scroller, an isometric board, or a three-dimensional dungeon without changing that loading shape.
the-codename-one-game-builder.captureNarration: The editor exposes hierarchy, assets, canvas, and properties in one place. The board and dungeon captures show that the format is not a platformer skin; each mode has its own projection and preview while retaining the same project data model.
the-codename-one-game-builder.victoryTitle: Draw what changes often; code what makes it yours
the-codename-one-game-builder.victoryNarration: The developer can review a level as data, regenerate object bindings safely, reuse standard Codename One UI for menus and heads-up displays, and stream neighboring regions when the world grows beyond one scene.
the-codename-one-game-builder.outroTitle: Which game should this scene model build next?
the-codename-one-game-builder.outroPrompt: Choose a platformer, board game, dungeon, racer, or open world.
the-codename-one-game-builder.endScreenVideoTitle: Build a Blackjack Game in Java With Game Builder

java-to-a-native-linux-app.youtubeTitle: Compile Java Into a Native Linux ELF With No JVM
java-to-a-native-linux-app.openingTitle:
A Linux user should launch
the application, not a runtime
java-to-a-native-linux-app.thumbnailSubtitle: ParparVM, Zig, and an old glibc target produce one self-contained x64 or arm64 executable.
java-to-a-native-linux-app.openingNarration: Codename One builds native apps in Java from one codebase. Today, the Linux target becomes one self-contained executable for x64 or arm64 with no JVM on the user's machine.
java-to-a-native-linux-app.humanBeat: A developer wants to hand a Linux user one executable that launches normally instead of asking which JVM, distribution package, or installer they have.
java-to-a-native-linux-app.visualIdentity: Java bytecode moves through ParparVM and Zig, sheds unreachable code and resources into a compact native ELF, then runs on two architectures.
java-to-a-native-linux-app.bespokeVisualization: The compilation chain keeps system GTK libraries visible beside the self-contained application so portability claims remain precise.

heroJourney.identity: Codename One and native Linux desktop delivery are introduced.
heroJourney.problem: A Java archive needs a JVM, while native Linux delivery faces package managers, architectures, libc compatibility, and desktop dependencies.
heroJourney.difficulty: A binary linked on a new distribution can fail on an older one, resources must travel with the executable, and graphics, media, browser, storage, and networking need native implementations.
heroJourney.intervention: ParparVM translates reachable bytecode to C and Zig compiles it against an old glibc target into one resource-embedded ELF.
heroJourney.victory: The developer ships an x64 or arm64 native application that launches without a target JVM and uses the Linux desktop stack.

resolutionMap.solution: ParparVM bytecode-to-C compilation
resolutionMap.proof: the local-linux-device Maven build and resulting ELF

resolutionMap.solution: Zig cross-compilation against roughly glibc 2.17
GTK3, Cairo, Pango, EGL, GStreamer, WebKitGTK, and system services

java-to-a-native-linux-app.codeExcerpt:
mvn -pl common package \
    -Dcodename1.platform=linux \
    -Dcodename1.buildTarget=local-linux-device cn1:build

# Select the target
linux.arch=arm64
# Alpine uses musl instead of glibc
linux.libc=musl
# Keep symbols while debugging
linux.debug=true
java-to-a-native-linux-app.problemTitle: Linux portability has more than one moving target
java-to-a-native-linux-app.problemNarration: A Java archive still needs a compatible JVM. A native binary built against a new glibc may refuse to start on an older distribution. Then x64, arm64, musl, graphics, media, browser, printing, storage, and desktop services all enter the delivery problem.

evidence.title: Linux binaries depend on a stable ABI
evidence.body: The glibc project tracks exported symbol versions because binaries rely on the C library ABI available on the target system.

evidence.title: Zig supports explicit cross-compilation targets
evidence.body: Zig's build tooling can select architecture, operating system, and ABI so one host can produce binaries for another Linux target.

java-to-a-native-linux-app.interventionTitle: Compile the application, then target old Linux
java-to-a-native-linux-app.interventionNarration: ParparVM translates reachable Java and Kotlin bytecode to C and removes code the application never calls. Zig compiles that C for x64 or arm64 against an old glibc baseline, while themes, images, localization, and fonts are embedded directly in the ELF.
java-to-a-native-linux-app.proofTitle: The build is an ordinary Maven target
java-to-a-native-linux-app.proofNarration: The local Linux goal produces a native binary for the selected architecture. Build hints choose arm64, the musl variant for Alpine, or retained symbols for debugging; the default remains an optimized glibc executable.
java-to-a-native-linux-app.captureNarration: The resulting application renders a Codename One form through GTK3 and Cairo, a glTF model through the portable three-dimensional API and OpenGL ES, and CSS gradients through the native Linux drawing stack.
java-to-a-native-linux-app.victoryNarration: The developer ships the same application logic as a native Linux ELF, chooses the architecture and C library deliberately, and requires no JVM installation or bundled runtime on the target machine.
java-to-a-native-linux-app.outroTitle: Which Linux target should we test next?
java-to-a-native-linux-app.outroPrompt: Name the distribution, architecture, and desktop stack you deploy to.
java-to-a-native-linux-app.endScreenVideoTitle: Java AOT vs JIT: How ParparVM Reached HotSpot Performance
